song_description: Commissioned by the SDSO in 2009. Honors the relationship between the Black Hills and the Lakota people. Integrates the traditional Lakota song 'Heart Butte Special' into an orchestral texture. In the concluding moments, the singers become the leading voice.
recording_location: Mary W. Sommervold Hall, Washington Pavilion / Hamre Recital Hall, Augustana University, Sioux Falls SD
liner_notes: Commissioned by the SDSO in 2009. Integrates the traditional Lakota song 'Heart Butte Special' into an orchestral texture featuring the Creekside Singers (Lakota drumming group from Pine Ridge Reservation). In the concluding moments, the singers become the leading voice, driving the music to its rousing conclusion.

song_description: Composed for the SDSO and Dakota flutist Bryan Akipa. Describes Clear Lake in South Dakota, its deep spirituality. Soundscape suggests waves lapping, leaves rustling, wind chimes, distant thunder, frogs, barn swallows.
recording_location: Mary W. Sommervold Hall, Washington Pavilion / Hamre Recital Hall, Augustana University, Sioux Falls SD
liner_notes: Composed for the SDSO and Dakota flutist Bryan Akipa by SDSO principal oboist Jeffrey Paul. Paul writes of Clear Lake: 'deep spirituality,' sensing 'with heightened awareness many generations of previous inhabitants.' Soundscape: waves lapping at the shore, leaves rustling, wind chimes, distant thunder, occasional frog or barn swallow.

featured_performers: Jeffrey Paul (guitar); Robert Erhard (cello); Sharon Mautner-Rodgers (cello); Creekside Singers
song_description: Originally composed on a pawn shop electric guitar. Described as both contemplative and a primal cry into the beautiful and endless open spaces of the natural world.
recording_location: Mary W. Sommervold Hall, Washington Pavilion / Hamre Recital Hall, Augustana University, Sioux Falls SD
liner_notes: Originally composed by Jeffrey Paul on a pawn shop electric guitar. Paul describes the work as both contemplative and 'a primal cry into the beautiful and endless open spaces of the natural world.'

featured_performers: Bryan Akipa (Dakota flute); Doosook Kim (violin); Daniel Kitchens (horn); Mark Nelson (trumpet); Creekside Singers
song_description: Inspired by the discovery that Amazing Grace is a traditional song for Lakota drumming groups. Created while composer-in-residence at SDSO. Wiprud writes: 'Perhaps this work captures what we're doing here — finding common ground as equals between two long musical traditions.'
recording_location: Mary W. Sommervold Hall, Washington Pavilion / Hamre Recital Hall, Augustana University, Sioux Falls SD
liner_notes: Arrangement inspired by Theodore Wiprud's discovery that Amazing Grace is a traditional song for Lakota drumming groups, created while Wiprud was composer-in-residence at SDSO. Wiprud: 'Perhaps this work captures what we're doing here - finding common ground as equals between two long musical traditions.'

track_title: Can't Get You Off My Mind
As Long as You Come Home Tonite
Please, Don't Be Mad at Me
Lakota National Anthem (Flag Song) and Veterans' Song
featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: Flag Song — The President's Flag will stand forever. Veterans' Song — often follows the Flag Song at the raising or lowering of the U.S. flag.
lakota_lyrics: Tunkaśila yapi, tawapaha ki han oihankesni (he) nanjin kte lo. / Iyolilateya oyate ki han wicicagin kte ca. Iecamun welo. | Heyuha natan pe / Tunkaśila yapi tawapaha ca / Heyuha natan pelo. / Lakota hokśila (he) ohitika ca / Heyuha natan pe. Tunkaśila yapi tawapaha ca. / Heyuha natan pelo.
english_translation: The President's Flag will stand forever. Under it, the people will grow: so I do this. | They are charging. The U.S. flag, they are charging. The Lakota warriors are brave. They are charging. The U.S. flag, they are charging.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
prior_release: Originally released on Porcupine Singers at Ring Thunder, Volume 1 (CR-8006)
liner_notes: Flag Song: 'The President's Flag will stand forever. Under it, the people will grow: so I do this.' Veterans' Song follows the Flag Song at flag ceremonies: 'The Lakota warriors are brave. They are charging.' Originally released on Porcupine Singers at Ring Thunder, Volume 1 (CR-8006).

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: A song made for the Battle of Little Big Horn (1876).
lakota_lyrics: Kola tokile, kola tokile, kola ceyapelo. / Waziyata ki cizape. / Kola tokile, kola tokile, kola ceyapelo.
english_translation: Friend, where are you? Friend, they are crying. They are fighting up north. Friend, where are you? Friend, they are crying.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Commemorates the Battle of Little Big Horn (1876). Sung in the first person, conveying grief and disorientation of warriors: 'Friend, where are you? Friend, they are crying. They are fighting up north.'

track_title: Our Friends Came With The Soldiers
featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: A song made for the Eastern Lakota who scouted for the U.S. cavalry against the Western Lakota.
lakota_lyrics: Wayankiye kola Lakotako natan hiye kola nita kola nape wayelo.
english_translation: Behold, our friends came with the soldiers, so I chased my friend away.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Reflects the painful internal divisions during the Indian Wars: Eastern Lakota who scouted for the U.S. cavalry against the Western Lakota. Context of ambivalence and historical reckoning: 'Behold, our friends came with the soldiers, so I chased my friend away.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: A Sneak-Up Dance for a warrior who was wounded in battle. One of the warriors was knocked off his horse and the others picked him up and are carrying him back.
lakota_lyrics: Heyuha manipe. / Eca Lakota hokśila waśośeyape. / Heyuha manipe.
english_translation: Our friends, they are walking with him. In the middle of the battle they are crying as they carry him back.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Warrior dance song for a warrior knocked from his horse and carried back by comrades. Booklet context: 'One of the warriors was knocked off his horse and the others picked him up and are carrying him back.' Translation: 'Our friends, they are walking with him. In the middle of the battle they are crying as they carry him back.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: This song exemplifies the patriotism of the Lakota.
lakota_lyrics: Lakota hokśila iya sica tamakoce ki ota iyacuca ekta wicaceyahe.
english_translation: The Lakota soldiers took the Germans' land so they are still crying.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Reflects Lakota patriotism during WWI coexisting with ongoing cultural suppression. Translation: 'The Lakota soldiers took the Germans' land so they are still crying.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: One of the Porcupine Singers' standard songs.
lakota_lyrics: Iyuśkinyan waunci yunkan winyanla ki nawizina isikcin amakinape.
english_translation: We were having a good time dancing, so my little woman got jealous and took me out of the dance hall.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Described in the booklet as 'one of the Porcupine Singers' standard songs.' Social/humorous in character. Translation: 'We were having a good time dancing, so my little woman got jealous and took me out of the dance hall.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: The leader of a successful raiding party barks like a dog four times in advance of the returning party.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Raiding party victory song: the leader barks like a dog four times in advance of the returning party. Lakota text: 'Sunka ho pelo' ('The voice of the dog'). One of the shorter traditional songs on this album.

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: A memorial song made for One Feather.
lakota_lyrics: Nita kola ake nicanpe Wiyaka Wanjila nita kola ake nicanpe.
english_translation: They are fighting over your friend (he has fallen). Your friend One Feather, they are fighting over him.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: A memorial song specifically composed for a warrior named One Feather. Demonstrates the Lakota tradition of named memorial songs for individual warriors. Translation: 'They are fighting over your friend (he has fallen). Your friend One Feather, they are fighting over him.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: A song of encouragement sung for a person having a birthday. The Lakota name of the person being honored is inserted at the beginning of the song.
lakota_lyrics: (Name) Blihic' iyayo nita kola ki ahihuni. / Blihic' iyayo mada sitomi otehikelo.
english_translation: (Name) take heart, your friends are coming saying this. Take heart, there are hard times all over the land.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: A living ceremonial song used at birthday celebrations. The Lakota name of the honoree is inserted at the song's opening, making each performance a personalized tribute. Translation: '(Name) take heart, your friends are coming saying this. Take heart, there are hard times all over the land.'

featured_performers: Porcupine Singers (lead singer: James Clairmont; drum keeper: Severt Young Bear Sr.)
song_description: This song expresses the sentiment of many dancers caught between their desire to follow the traditional ways and the pressures of the modern world.
lakota_lyrics: Lakol wicoh' an kin tohanl ablustag ki Otokipi wani cin kte lo. / Waci wicasa heya yaun ca tamunka sni velo.
english_translation: Whenever I quit my Native ways, there will be no more happiness. The dancers are saying this. It is difficult to take.
recording_location: Ring Thunder Wacipi, Rosebud Reservation, South Dakota (live, 1977)
liner_notes: Regarded as one of the most culturally significant songs on this album for its direct engagement with identity and cultural survival. Translation: 'Whenever I quit my Native ways, there will be no more happiness. The dancers are saying this. It is difficult to take.'

recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: World Premiere Recording. Version III ('final') of the Hiawatha Melodrama, premiered by PostClassical Ensemble, Angel Gil-Ordonez, and Kevin Deas at Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, 1 March 2013. Text from Longfellow's The Song of Hiawatha (Prologue and Chapters 10, 11, 12, 17, 20, 22). Horowitz expanded this version to include Dvorak's American Suite and Violin Sonatina, creating a continuous 35-minute narrative.

recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Music adapts the Larghetto from Dvorak's Violin Sonatina (composed in Iowa, 1893) and portions of the Largo from the New World Symphony. The Larghetto (as Beckerman documented) is a portrait of Minnehaha ('Laughing Water'), Hiawatha's wife - Dvorak alternates major/minor modes to depict her moods. Midway through, he depicts the 53-foot Minnehaha Falls orchestrated with harp arpeggios.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: III. Hiawatha's Wedding Feast
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Music extracted from the Scherzo of the New World Symphony, which Dvorak testified begins with stomping, whirling music inspired by the Dance of Pau-Puk-Keewis at Hiawatha's wedding. The alignments with the song of Chibiabos and the tale told by Iagoo are Beckerman's scholarship.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: IV. The Death of Minnehaha
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Music begins with a C-sharp minor passage from American Suite movement III in Dvorak's 'Indian' style, continuing with a C-sharp minor passage from the New World Symphony Largo. Czech writer Katerina Emingerova singled out the forest funeral as a key source passage for the Largo.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: V. The Hunting of Pau-Puk-Keewis
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Music extracted from the New World Symphony finale (a Beckerman alignment). Certain Longfellow lines are here sung. Beckerman: 'As soon as Dvorak returned to Bohemia in 1895, he composed tone poems based on ballads of K.J. Erben, setting down the poem, line by line, beneath the music.'

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: VI. Epilogue — Hiawatha's Departure
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Music recalls the American Suite theme from Part Three and passages from the New World Symphony. Horowitz: the singular ending - dirge, apotheosis, diminuendo - can only be programmatic. 'A plausible guess that Dvorak's reading of this elegiac ending, with its timpani taps, was inspired by the ending of Longfellow's poem.'

track_title: Goin' Home (after Dvořák Symphony No. 9 'From the New World': II. Largo)
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Text by William Arms Fisher (1861-1948), a student of Dvorak at the National Conservatory, who adapted the famous Largo melody in 1922. The song became associated in the U.S. with spiritual tradition and homecoming - among the most recognized borrowings from a classical symphony in American popular culture.

track_title: Violin Sonatina in G major Op. 100 B. 183: II. Larghetto (1893)
genre_tags: chamber; violin; piano; Czech; American period
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Previously released on Naxos 8.554413 (c) 2001. Composed in Spillville, Iowa, 1893. Popularized by Fritz Kreisler as the Indian Lament; Beckerman documented it as a portrait of Minnehaha. Zhou Qian plays a 1757 J.B. Guadagnini, generously loaned by Mr. and Mrs. Rin Kei Mei.

track_title: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 4. Poco andante in F major (1894)
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Composed in Prague between Dvorak's stints in Manhattan; American and Bohemian styles juxtaposed. Fourth Humoresque opens with what sounds like a foreshadowing of Gershwin's Porgy and Bess (composed four decades later).

track_title: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 7. Poco lento e grazioso in G flat major (1894)
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: The Seventh Humoresque hums a dance tune so familiar in the U.S. that many Americans assume an American composed it. Dvorak's American and Bohemian styles are interwoven throughout the Humoresques.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': I. Moderato (1894–95)
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Begun in New York just after the New World Symphony premiere; scored for solo piano then orchestrated 1895. In Spillville, Iowa, Dvorak listened to interracial Kickapoo Medicine Show musicians intermingling Native American dances with banjo and guitar. Willa Cather's 'immeasurable yearning of all flat lands' defines this suite. Movement I (Moderato) establishes clean, unadorned American sonority.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': II. Molto vivace
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Movement II (Molto vivace). The American Suite's defining quality is simplicity - serene speech, shunning compositional virtuosity.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': III. Allegretto
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Movement III (Allegretto). A jaunty dance not far removed from the world of stride piano. The elegiac Indianist refrain from this movement (C-sharp minor) is extracted for the Hiawatha Melodrama's Part Three (The Death of Minnehaha).

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': IV. Andante
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Movement IV (Andante). Evokes the vacant Iowa landscape Dvorak found 'sometimes very sad, sad to despair.' Prairie vacancy mates with cakewalk in the American Suite.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': V. Allegro
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Movement V (Allegro). An A major 'Indian' tune turns into an A major minstrel song - fusing the Indianist and African-American musical traditions Dvorak encountered during his American residency.

recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Navajo War Dance No. 2 (1904), dedicated to John Kirkpatrick (later to champion Ives' Concord Sonata). With dissonance and rhythmic bite (remarkably progressive for 1904), it suggests a New World Bartok. Farwell's lifelong reverence for Native American culture began in childhood when he lived in a Native American village on Lake Superior.

track_title: Mesa and Plain Op. 20 No. 2: Pawnee Horses (1905)
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: Based on an Omaha song Farwell considered so rhythmically complex it could not be performed by 'any known singer except an Indian.' Piano version (Op. 20), barely a minute long.

featured_performers: University of Texas Chamber Singers; Michael Worfe (tenor solo); James Morrow (conductor)
genre_tags: choral; American; Indianist; Pawnee; a cappella
recording_location: Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, College Park MD (tracks 1-7, 9-15, 17); Potton Hall, Westleton UK (track 8); Toronto Centre for the Arts, Canada (track 16); Bates Recital Hall, Austin TX (track 18)
liner_notes: World Premiere Recording. In 1937, Farwell created this eight-part a cappella choral version for John Finlay Williamson's Westminster Choir - described as 'an American choral masterpiece.' Based on the same Omaha source as Op. 20 No. 2, expanded into a full choral work.

song_description: Orchestral rhapsody sung in the Lakota language, inspired by writings of Charles Alexander Eastman, composed in honor of Lloyd Running Bear Sr. and all Lakota Indian warriors. Each movement depicts a legendary Lakota warrior and his deeds.
recording_location: Mary W. Sommervold Hall, Washington Pavilion / Hamre Recital Hall, Augustana University, Sioux Falls SD
liner_notes: Orchestral rhapsody inspired by the writings of Charles Alexander Eastman, composed in honor of Lloyd Running Bear, Sr. and all Lakota Indian warriors. Sung in the Lakota language. Six movements each depicting a legendary Lakota warrior: I. Introduction; II. Red Cloud (Mahpiya uta); III. Two-Strike (Num KahpA); IV. Gall (Phizi); V. Crazy Horse (Thasunke Witko); VI. Sitting Bull (Thathanka Iyotake).
